metadata.summary: Low-Rank Adaptation and Quantized LoRA for efficient fine-tuning of open-source models on custom datasets with limited GPU memory.

content.title: LoRA and QLoRA: Low-Rank Adaptation for Efficient Fine-tuning
content.summary: LoRA (Low-Rank Adaptation) freezes base model weights and injects small trainable matrices into attention layers, reducing trainable parameters from 100% to 0.1-1% with minimal quality loss. QLoRA extends this by loading the base model in 4-bit quantized form and training LoRA adapters in fp16, enabling fine-tuning of 70B models on a single 48GB GPU. Both techniques enable task-specific adapter training that can be swapped at inference time without reloading the base model.

why.p: Full fine-tuning of an 8B parameter model requires ~28GB of GPU memory; a 70B model requires 280GB — prohibitive for most teams. Fine-tuning costs are dominated by memory, not compute. LoRA sidesteps this by freezing the base and training only 0.1-1% of parameters, reducing memory to 16GB for 8B and 140GB for 70B. QLoRA further reduces these to 6-8GB and 40-48GB respectively by quantizing the base to 4-bit and training the small LoRA adapters in full precision.

Beyond memory, LoRA enables task specialization: train one adapter for customer support, another for code, another for summarization, all sharing the same base model. Adapters are small (1-10MB), versionable, and can be deployed independently. They integrate seamlessly with production inference via PEFT (Parameter-Efficient Fine-Tuning).

list.item: Fine-tuning an open-source model (LLaMA, Mistral, Qwen, Phi) on a custom dataset with limited GPU memory.
Training domain-specific adapters that can be swapped at inference time without reloading the base model.
Producing multiple task-specific adapters from one base model (customer support, code, summarization).
Reducing fine-tuning cost vs. full fine-tuning: QLoRA brings a 70B model into a single 48GB GPU.
Creating a task-specific model when OpenAI fine-tuning is too expensive or the data must stay on-prem.
Fewer than 500-1000 domain-specific examples — few-shot prompting or RAG will outperform a poorly-trained adapter.
The goal is injecting new factual knowledge — LoRA improves style/format, not factual recall; use RAG for knowledge.
The target task is simple enough for a prompt template with a smaller base model.
The team lacks GPU access (even a single A100 or consumer 3090/4090); CPU-only training is impractical.
Production requires strict reproducibility; adapter merging and quantization introduce non-determinism.

section.name: lora-core-concept
section.p: LoRA freezes the base model and injects small trainable matrices into attention layers. The original weight W (dimensions d × k) is augmented by the product of two low-rank matrices B and A, where B is d × r and A is r × k. Only B and A are trained, and r is much smaller than both d and k (typically 8-64 vs. hundreds or thousands).

rule.statement: LoRA configurations MUST specify four core parameters: (1) rank (r), typically 8-64 depending on task complexity; (2) alpha (scaling factor), usually equal to rank; (3) target_modules, the attention layers to adapt (q_proj, k_proj, v_proj, o_proj, plus MLP layers gate_proj, up_proj, down_proj for instruction-following); (4) dropout, typically 0.05-0.1 for regularization. Configurations MUST NOT target fewer than four attention layers; targeting only q and v (a common shortcut) leaves 40-60% of trainable capacity unused.
rule.rationale: Each parameter has a specific purpose: rank controls the expressiveness of the adapter (higher rank = more capacity but more parameters); alpha scales the LoRA contribution to prevent catastrophic forgetting; target_modules determines which layers benefit from adaptation (attention layers are more sensitive than feed-forward layers); dropout prevents overfitting on small datasets. Incomplete target coverage is a major failure mode — the model appears to train cleanly but learns less than expected.

example.title: LoRA rank selection: task complexity
example.code:
# Simple task (classification, style transfer): rank 8-16 lora_config_simple = LoraConfig( r=8, lora_alpha=8, target_modules=["q_proj", "k_proj", "v_proj", "o_proj"], lora_dropout=0.05, bias="none", task_type="CAUSAL_LM", )
# Complex task (instruction-following, reasoning): rank 32-64 lora_config_complex = LoraConfig( r=32, lora_alpha=32, target_modules=["q_proj", "k_proj", "v_proj", "o_proj", "gate_proj", "up_proj", "down_proj"], lora_dropout=0.1, bias="none", task_type="CAUSAL_LM", )
example.note: Simple tasks saturate with lower rank; complex tasks require more capacity. MLP layers are targeted for instruction-following to enable multi-step reasoning.

example.title: Incomplete target modules
example.code:
# Shortcut: only target q_proj and v_proj lora_config_incomplete = LoraConfig( r=32, lora_alpha=32, target_modules=["q_proj", "v_proj"], # Missing k, o, MLP layers task_type="CAUSAL_LM", )
# Result: model trains cleanly but learns less than expected # because 40-60% of attention capacity is frozen
example.note: Appears to work but underperforms. Always target all attention projections and MLP layers for instruction-following tasks.

section.name: qlora-quantization
section.p: QLoRA loads the base model in 4-bit precision using NF4 (Normal Float 4) quantization, trains LoRA adapters in fp16, and uses paged optimizers to handle memory spikes. The combination enables a 70B model to fit in 40-48GB of GPU memory — a 5-7x reduction from full fine-tuning.

rule.statement: QLoRA deployments MUST use exactly this quantization stack: (1) load_in_4bit=True with bnb_4bit_quant_type="nf4", (2) bnb_4bit_compute_dtype=torch.bfloat16 (or torch.float16 on older GPUs), (3) bnb_4bit_use_double_quant=True (quantize the quantization constants themselves), (4) call prepare_model_for_kbit_training BEFORE get_peft_model (reversing the order silently produces wrong gradients), (5) use optim="paged_adamw_8bit" to handle memory spikes in the optimizer. Quantized base models MUST NOT be merged with adapters in production; keep adapters separate to avoid quality degradation.
rule.rationale: Each quantization parameter has measurable impact: NF4 is optimal for LLM inference and training; double quantization saves ~25% additional memory; bfloat16 has wider dynamic range than float16 on A100/H100 and avoids overflow. Calling prepare_model_for_kbit_training in the wrong order breaks gradient computation silently — the model appears to train but gradients are corrupted. Merging quantized adapters causes quality loss because the merge operation assumes the base is in full precision.

example.title: QLoRA configuration: 70B on 48GB
example.code:
import torch from transformers import BitsAndBytesConfig, AutoModelForCausalLM from peft import LoraConfig, get_peft_model, prepare_model_for_kbit_training
# Step 1: Configure quantization bnb_config = BitsAndBytesConfig( load_in_4bit=True, bnb_4bit_quant_type="nf4", bnb_4bit_compute_dtype=torch.bfloat16, bnb_4bit_use_double_quant=True, )
# Step 2: Load base model in 4-bit model = AutoModelForCausalLM.from_pretrained( "meta-llama/Llama-3.1-70B", quantization_config=bnb_config, device_map="auto", )
# Step 3: Prepare for training (MUST be before get_peft_model) model = prepare_model_for_kbit_training(model)
# Step 4: Configure LoRA lora_config = LoraConfig( r=16, lora_alpha=16, target_modules=["q_proj", "k_proj", "v_proj", "o_proj"], lora_dropout=0.05, bias="none", task_type="CAUSAL_LM", )
# Step 5: Apply LoRA model = get_peft_model(model, lora_config)
# Check trainable params: ~13M out of 70B = 0.02% model.print_trainable_parameters()
example.note: Precise order is critical. Memory usage: ~40-48GB on A100 or H100. Training speed: ~0.8x of full fp16 due to quantization overhead, but memory savings make it viable.

section.name: training-setup
section.p: LoRA and QLoRA training use the standard Hugging Face training loop via SFTTrainer (Supervised Fine-Tuning Trainer) from the TRL library. The key is proper hyperparameter selection based on dataset size and task complexity.

rule.statement: LoRA training configurations MUST be selected based on three factors: (1) Dataset size: <500 examples → use lower learning rate (5e-5), >10K examples → use higher LR (2e-4); (2) Task complexity: simple classification → rank 8, instruction-following → rank 32-64; (3) GPU memory: enable gradient checkpointing (30% memory savings, 15% throughput cost) on 8B+ models. Training MUST include: per_device_train_batch_size=2-4, gradient_accumulation_steps=4-8 (to simulate larger batches), lr_scheduler_type="cosine" (better convergence than linear), warmup_ratio=0.03 (3% of steps), fp16=True or bf16=True (depends on GPU).
rule.rationale: Learning rate scales with dataset size: small datasets require conservative LR to avoid overfitting; large datasets benefit from higher LR for faster convergence. Gradient checkpointing trades memory for throughput — always worth it on 8B+ models because memory is the bottleneck. Cosine learning rate decay provides better final loss than linear. Warmup prevents divergence in the first steps.

example.title: QLoRA training: dataset size-based hyperparams
example.code:
from transformers import TrainingArguments from trl import SFTTrainer
# Hyperparameters vary by dataset size dataset_size = len(dataset) # Example: 2000 examples
if dataset_size < 500: learning_rate = 5e-5 epochs = 5 elif dataset_size < 5000: learning_rate = 2e-4 epochs = 3 else: learning_rate = 1e-4 epochs = 1
training_args = TrainingArguments( output_dir="./lora_output", per_device_train_batch_size=2, gradient_accumulation_steps=4, num_train_epochs=epochs, learning_rate=learning_rate, logging_steps=10, save_steps=100, optim="paged_adamw_8bit", warmup_ratio=0.03, lr_scheduler_type="cosine", bf16=True, # Use bf16 on A100/H100 )
# Enable gradient checkpointing to save memory model.gradient_checkpointing_enable()
trainer = SFTTrainer( model=model, args=training_args, train_dataset=dataset, tokenizer=tokenizer, max_seq_length=2048, dataset_text_field="text", )
trainer.train()
example.note: Learning rate and epochs are calibrated to dataset size. Gradient checkpointing is enabled to fit larger models. Cosine scheduler improves final loss vs. linear.

section.name: saving-and-loading-adapters
section.p: LoRA adapters are small (~1-10MB) and are saved separately from the base model. Loading requires the base model to be loaded first, then the adapter is attached via PEFT.

rule.statement: LoRA adapter saving and loading MUST follow this pattern: (1) call model.save_pretrained("path") to save only the adapter (LoRA matrices and config), not the base model; (2) load via AutoPeftModelForCausalLM.from_pretrained("path") which automatically loads the adapter and merges it into the base for inference; (3) do NOT attempt to load an adapter path directly as a full model — this will raise a dimension mismatch error. For production deployment, merge the adapter into the base via model.merge_and_unload() to produce a single model file, but keep the separate adapter for versioning and A/B testing.
rule.rationale: Adapters are designed to be small and versionable; saving just the LoRA matrices (typically 1-10MB) is the intended workflow. Merging should only happen for final deployment to a production service that cannot swap adapters. Keeping separate adapters enables task switching and A/B testing without reloading the base.

example.title: Saving adapter and loading for inference
example.code:
# After training, save the adapter model.save_pretrained("./customer_support_adapter") tokenizer.save_pretrained("./customer_support_adapter")
# Loading for inference from peft import AutoPeftModelForCausalLM
adapter_model = AutoPeftModelForCausalLM.from_pretrained( "./customer_support_adapter", device_map="auto", torch_dtype=torch.float16, )
# Inference input_ids = tokenizer.encode("Customer: How do I reset my password?", return_tensors="pt") output = adapter_model.generate(input_ids, max_length=512) print(tokenizer.decode(output[0]))
# For production, merge the adapter into the base merged_model = adapter_model.merge_and_unload() merged_model.save_pretrained("./customer_support_model_merged")
example.note: Adapter is 1-10MB; merged model is full base size. Keep adapter for versioning, use merged for production deployment.

section.name: advanced-techniques
section.p: DoRA (Weight-Decomposed LoRA) is a 2024 variant that decomposes weight updates into magnitude and direction, improving performance over standard LoRA. Multiple adapters can be trained and swapped on the same base model.

rule.statement: DoRA (use_dora=True) provides measurable improvements over LoRA (2-5% quality gain on some benchmarks) but is incompatible with some PEFT versions and quantization backends — always test in a development environment before committing to it. Multiple task-specific adapters MUST be trained on the same base model and kept separate; do not merge multiple adapters into a single model. Adapter scaling (set_adapter_scale) affects all LoRA layers uniformly; there is no per-layer control without custom patching.
rule.rationale: DoRA is a strict improvement over LoRA in theory but has implementation issues in some libraries; field testing is essential. Multiple adapters on one base enable rapid task switching without retraining. Uniform scaling is a limitation but sufficient for most use cases.

example.title: DoRA: improved LoRA variant
example.code:
from peft import LoraConfig
# Standard LoRA lora_config_standard = LoraConfig( r=16, lora_alpha=16, target_modules=["q_proj", "k_proj", "v_proj", "o_proj"], task_type="CAUSAL_LM", )
# DoRA variant (2024) dora_config = LoraConfig( r=16, lora_alpha=16, target_modules=["q_proj", "k_proj", "v_proj", "o_proj"], use_dora=True, # Enable DoRA task_type="CAUSAL_LM", )
# DoRA generally improves final loss by 2-5% on complex tasks # but requires testing in your environment before production
example.note: DoRA decomposes weight updates into magnitude and direction. Measurable improvement but requires library compatibility check.

example.title: Multiple task-specific adapters
example.code:
# Train adapter 1 for customer support model = get_peft_model(base_model, lora_config_support) trainer.train() model.save_pretrained("./adapter_support")
# Train adapter 2 for code generation base_model_fresh = AutoModelForCausalLM.from_pretrained("meta-llama/Llama-3.1-8B") model = get_peft_model(base_model_fresh, lora_config_code) trainer.train() model.save_pretrained("./adapter_code")
# Load and switch between adapters at inference from peft import PeftModel
base = AutoModelForCausalLM.from_pretrained("meta-llama/Llama-3.1-8B") model_support = PeftModel.from_pretrained(base, "./adapter_support") model_code = PeftModel.from_pretrained(base, "./adapter_code")
# Each adapter is 1-10MB; switching is instantaneous
example.note: One base model, multiple specialized adapters. Each adapter is tiny and versionable. Switch at inference time without reloading the base.

section.name: best-practices
section.p: LoRA and QLoRA have consistent failure modes. These practices are derived from 100+ fine-tuning runs across production systems.

rule.statement: LoRA deployments MUST target all attention layers (q, k, v, o) plus MLP layers (gate, up, down) for instruction-following tasks. Parameter count at init MUST be logged and reviewed; if trainable params exceed 2% of total, the rank is too high and will exceed GPU memory during training. Validation loss MUST be monitored for overfitting; if val loss increases for 5 consecutive steps, stop training immediately (early stopping). Dataset token length distribution MUST be validated before training; sequences truncated at max_seq_length mid-sentence silently degrade quality.
rule.rationale: Incomplete target coverage is common and hard to diagnose. Trainable parameter overflow is invisible until OOM during training — logging at init catches it early. Early stopping prevents wasting GPU time on diverging runs. Token distribution validation is overlooked but critical — truncation artifacts poison the training signal.

rule.statement: Always enable gradient checkpointing (model.gradient_checkpointing_enable()) before training on 8B+ models — saves ~30% memory at ~15% throughput cost. Use bf16=True instead of fp16=True on A100/H100 — bf16 has wider dynamic range and avoids overflow on long runs. For hyperparameter selection, use rank=16 as a safe default for general tasks; only go lower (rank=8) for simple classification or higher (rank=32-64) for complex reasoning. Alpha MUST equal rank (common practice, avoids tuning a second hyperparameter).
rule.rationale: Gradient checkpointing is always worth the throughput trade-off on memory-constrained models. bf16 is strictly better than fp16 on modern hardware. Rank selection has well-established guidelines by task complexity. Alpha=rank removes a hyperparameter.

rule.statement: Evaluate adapters with temperature=0 on a fixed held-out set before and after training — this isolates the adapter's effect from sampling noise. Avoid merging adapters in production unless absolutely necessary for deployment convenience; merged adapters cannot be swapped or versioned. Keep adapter versions in a manifest (JSON with adapter_name, date, task, performance metrics) to enable rollback if a new adapter degrades production quality.
rule.rationale: Temperature=0 provides deterministic evaluation. Merging is a point of no return — it permanently alters the base model. Adapter manifests enable quick rollback if a new adapter causes issues in production.

section.name: failure-modes
section.p: LoRA and QLoRA have predictable failure modes that are preventable with upfront checks.

antipattern.description: Calling prepare_model_for_kbit_training AFTER get_peft_model instead of before.
antipattern.reason: The order matters for gradient computation. Reversing it silently produces corrupted gradients — the training loss appears to decrease normally but the model learns the wrong patterns. Only discovered post-hoc when inference quality is poor.

antipattern.description: Training QLoRA on a CPU-only environment or a container without CUDA.
antipattern.reason: bitsandbytes (the quantization library) is CUDA-only and fails to import on CPU-only machines. The error is non-obvious and happens at import time, not at init. Gate training on torch.cuda.is_available().

antipattern.description: Using the dataset_text_field parameter in SFTTrainer with a column name that does not exist.
antipattern.reason: Raises a KeyError on the first forward pass, not at init. The error message is generic and does not reference the column name mismatch. Double-check column names in the dataset before launching training.

antipattern.description: Merging a 4-bit quantized adapter into the base model and expecting the same quality as keeping them separate.
antipattern.reason: Merging quantized adapters produces quality degradation because the merge operation assumes the base is in full precision. Keep adapters separate for production.

antipattern.description: Not setting the WANDB_API_KEY environment variable and wondering why training logs nowhere.
antipattern.reason: Weights & Biases integration requires the API key as an env var. If missing, training runs silently with no logging. Set WANDB_API_KEY or disable W&B logging before training.

section.name: memory-comparison
section.p: LoRA and QLoRA enable dramatic memory savings compared to full fine-tuning.

rule.statement: Memory and training time tradeoffs: Full fine-tuning requires storing gradients, activations, and parameters in fp32/fp16 — 28GB for 7B, 140GB for 70B. LoRA (0.1-1% trainable params in fp16) requires ~16GB for 7B, ~140GB for 70B (memory savings come from smaller effective batch size due to adapter size, not from parameter count). QLoRA (0.1-1% trainable in fp16, base in 4-bit) requires ~6-8GB for 7B, ~40-48GB for 70B. Training speed is roughly 1x for full fine-tuning, 1.2x for LoRA (adapter overhead), 0.8x for QLoRA (quantization overhead). For a fixed GPU, QLoRA enables 5-7x larger models than full fine-tuning.
rule.rationale: LoRA's memory savings come from training fewer parameters (smaller gradients and optimizer states). QLoRA's additional savings come from 4-bit quantization of the base model. Speed tradeoffs are acceptable because LoRA/QLoRA training runs on smaller GPUs, enabling many teams to train at all.

template.purpose: QLoRA training config, axolotl YAML templates, and hyperparameter selection guides.
